title: Metro and Monorail Projects: An Explanation!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Daily Thanthi of 24 April 2015 carried an illustrated report headed: “Two companies selected to implement Chennai Monorail Project; work order to be issued in May to the company quoting the lower amount.” This important report does not appear to have been carried by any other newspaper. Why do I call it important?

Immediately after taking office in 2011, the AIADMK government announced in the Governor's Address of 3 June: “This government will implement a Monorail project integrated with the existing transport systems of Chennai. In the first phase it will cover 111 kilometres and will gradually be extended to 300 kilometres. Appropriate studies will also be undertaken to implement Monorail in Coimbatore, Madurai and Tiruchirappalli to reduce congestion.”

Then, following Jayalalithaa's guidance, Finance Minister O. Panneerselvam, today's Chief Minister, said in the Budget of 4 August 2011 that funding needed to begin Monorail quickly would be identified within that year. Did they identify it? What became of the project?
Do you know why Jayalalithaa announced Monorail as soon as the AIADMK came to power?

Before the 2011 election, Governor Surjit Singh Barnala's Address of 7 January 2011 under the DMK government stated in paragraph 21 that the Rs 14,600-crore Chennai Metro Rail Project, covering forty-five kilometres with Japanese and Central assistance, had been approved and was being implemented rapidly to reduce congestion and provide safe, quick travel. It also said steps were under way to extend it to Tiruvottiyur at an estimated Rs 3,001 crore. The Metro was thus conceived and energetically implemented under the DMK government. Could that project be allowed to succeed? That is why Jayalalithaa announced Monorail and argued that it was better than Metro.

The Governor's Address of 30 January 2012 expressed happiness that tender work for Monorail was progressing quickly. The Budget of 26 March described four routes in detail. Dinamani reported on 16 June that there would be thirty-four stations and that a consultation chaired by the Minister had lasted more than two hours. Everyone in Tamil Nadu knows that Monorail was announced as a rival project because of Jayalalithaa's habitual hostility toward a Metro project introduced by the DMK government.

Although Jayalalithaa had opposed Metro and promised Monorail, the 2013 Governor's Address said Metro would be extended from Washermanpet to Tiruvottiyur. It presented this as though the AIADMK government had newly announced it, and newspapers prominently reported it. In truth, the Tiruvottiyur extension had already been announced under the DMK government.
On 3 June 2014, The Times of India wrote that Jayalalithaa's cherished Monorail project was refusing to move: the May deadline for bids had passed, yet contractors showed no interest.

Jayalalithaa later changed course. Last year's Governor's Address welcomed the successful implementation of Chennai Metro and said the Chief Minister had inaugurated its trial run, while not mentioning the once-praised Monorail even once. Though Jayalalithaa initially rejected Metro, when she met the Prime Minister last year she sought full Central assistance for its seventy-six-kilometre second phase, estimated at Rs 36,100 crore. Those who said Metro should not exist and refused to implement it later went to Delhi seeking money for that same project.

A Metro trial run from Ashok Nagar to Alandur took place in the first week of June last year. Metro officials said passenger service from Koyambedu to Alandur would begin as planned in October, after successful tests since February of tracks, electrical connections and signals. Yet October passed, and another six months passed. Is Metro running? No.

Jayalalithaa herself launched the trial run, and every newspaper carried it prominently on its front page. It was a Rs 14,600-crore project, yet she did not utter one word. Unlike projects she routinely inaugurated from the Secretariat by video link, she came in person. Had she spoken, would she not have had to say under which government and in which year the project began? So she waved a flag without a ceremonial address. The government note carefully omitted the government under which it began. It began under the DMK government when I was Chief Minister. How could they disclose that?

Although the trials succeeded, they have not opened Metro to the public and reduced congestion, because it began under the DMK and because Jayalalithaa is not Chief Minister. Dinamalar itself reported yesterday that, since Jayalalithaa left office, no building inauguration, project launch or foundation-laying had taken place in Tamil Nadu; Metro's opening was delayed; and even eighty-five renovated temples awaited consecration because those in government wanted everything done only after she returned as Chief Minister.

The AIADMK government also halted Metro expansion. Delhi Metro Rail Corporation Principal Adviser E. Sreedharan said in Chennai on 11 April 2012: “The Tamil Nadu government is making a very serious mistake by abandoning Phase Two of Chennai Metro. I wrote to the Chief Secretary three months ago and have received no reply. The decision is distressing. Chennai Metro Rail is carrying out its work professionally and well. Such a gap between phases is not good. Monorail can carry only one-fourth the number of Metro passengers. Monorail has proved unsuccessful in most cities. Metro alone suits mass travel. Monorail is ordinary and cannot carry many people. It is not a successful system; even in Japan it is used only in small towns. Great cities such as Chennai and Mumbai need Metro. Metro also costs less. Monorail costs fifty per cent more.” The AIADMK government ignored this experienced advice.

Just as they obstructed the Sethusamudram Project and the Maduravoyal-Port elevated road, they obstructed Metro expansion. Then they inaugurated its trial run and sought credit as though they had created it. But Tamil Nadu's people know very well under whose government Metro began.

It is in this setting that Daily Thanthi reported yesterday that two companies had been selected for Monorail, that the lower bidder would receive a work order in May, that Monorail was superior to Metro, and that it would begin running in Chennai within two years, citing Monorail circles.

Yet The Times of India of 3 April 2014 reported: “The difficulty in finding bidders for Chennai's Mono Rail Project has not stopped the State Government from planning a similar project in Coimbatore. The plan for a Mono Rail Project in Coimbatore appears far-fetched as the State Transport Department has not been able to find takers for its project in Chennai.” The truth is that under this government no one comes forward to execute its projects, and therefore none of its announcements becomes reality.

Upon taking office in 2011, Jayalalithaa set aside the DMK government's Metro and announced Monorail, claiming it was better. But Metro had advanced too far to abandon. At the same time, she did not want the DMK to receive credit. With that hostility, she still leaves Metro unopened even after its work is complete. The scheme announced when she came to power in May 2011 and in the Governor's Address of 3 June 2011 has not begun even four years later, yet they say Monorail will run in another two years. Whom are they trying to fool?
With affection,
M.K.
26 April 2015
